name: Kapre Giant
description: The Kapre Giant is a towering, tree-dwelling creature from Filipino folklore, often described as a large, dark, hairy humanoid. They are typically depicted smoking a large tobacco pipe, the scent of which is believed to disorient travelers. Kapres are solitary beings, often found perched atop large trees, particularly balete trees, known for their mystical properties. While not inherently malevolent, they are known to play tricks on people, leading them astray or causing mischief. They are said to have a strong connection to the natural world and are often seen as guardians of the forests. Encounters with Kapres are often interpreted as warnings or signs of impending misfortune. The size and strength of a Kapre Giant are immense, reflecting the power and mystery of the Philippine forests. They are a reminder of the unseen forces that exist within the natural world and the importance of respecting the spirits of the land. Stories about Kapres serve as cautionary tales, emphasizing the need to be mindful of one's surroundings and to avoid disturbing the balance of nature. Some believe that Kapres can be befriended, but only through respect and offerings. They are a significant part of Filipino cultural heritage, representing the enduring connection between humans and the environment.
subtitle: The towering guardian of the trees
appearance: The Kapre Giant is a massive humanoid, typically described as being over seven feet tall. They are covered in thick, dark hair, and their skin is often described as dark brown or black. They have piercing eyes and a wild, untamed appearance. Their most distinctive feature is the large tobacco pipe they constantly smoke, emitting a strong, pungent odor. They are often depicted wearing simple clothing, such as a bahag or no clothing at all. Their limbs are long and powerful, and their hands are large and calloused, perfect for gripping tree branches. The overall impression is one of immense power and primal connection to the forest.
habitat: Kapres are primarily found in large, old trees, particularly balete trees, located in forests, jungles, and even rural areas. They prefer secluded and undisturbed environments, away from human settlements. They are also said to inhabit abandoned houses or structures that have become overgrown with vegetation. The deep forest is their domain, where they can blend seamlessly with the shadows and foliage.
behavior: Kapres are solitary creatures, preferring to keep to themselves. They are not inherently evil, but they are known to play tricks on humans, often leading them astray or causing them to lose their way in the forest. They can also be territorial and protective of their domain, becoming aggressive towards those who disturb their peace. They are often seen as guardians of the forest, ensuring that the balance of nature is maintained. Some believe that Kapres can be befriended through respect and offerings, but this is rare. They are generally cautious and wary of humans, preferring to observe from a distance.

keyMyths.title: The Lost Traveler
keyMyths.description: A traveler lost in the forest encounters a Kapre Giant smoking his pipe, leading him deeper into the woods with illusions and disorientation. The traveler eventually finds his way back by offering tobacco and showing respect for the forest. The source is oral tradition.

sources.author: Maximo Ramos
sources.work: The Creatures of Philippine Lower Mythology
sources.description: A comprehensive study of various creatures from Philippine folklore.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of various creatures from Philippine folklore.

sources.author: Jordan Clark
sources.description: A collection of stories passed down through generations in the Philippines.
